3.4.1.learningObjective: Understand seed phrases — what they are, why they must never be shared

exerciseContent.instruction: Your seed phrase is the master key to your entire crypto life. This simulation tests whether you really understand what it is and how to handle it. One wrong habit here could cost everything.

steps.prompt: Your wallet just generated this seed phrase: 'legal winner thank year wave sausage worth useful legal winner thank.' What is this string of words, really?

options.text: A password that can be changed if compromised
options.explanation: A seed phrase cannot be changed. It's mathematically derived from the moment your wallet was created. If compromised, the only solution is to move all funds to a brand new wallet with a new seed phrase immediately.

options.text: A human-readable encoding of your private key — the single backup for your entire wallet
options.explanation: Exactly. The 12 or 24 words are a standardized encoding (BIP-39) of the number that generates your private keys. Anyone who has these words can reconstruct your wallet on any compatible app and access all your funds.

options.text: A recovery email code that Metamask uses to restore your account
options.explanation: There is no MetaMask account. There is no server. MetaMask is software — your wallet is mathematical. The seed phrase bypasses all software entirely — it reconstructs your keys from pure math.

steps.microExplanation: BIP-39 is a standard used by almost all crypto wallets. Your 12 or 24 words map to a specific large number that generates your private key. Same words, anywhere, any wallet app — same wallet.

steps.prompt: Where should you store your seed phrase? Rank these options from best to worst and choose the single safest approach.

options.text: Written on paper, stored in a fireproof safe at home with a copy in a separate location
options.explanation: This is the gold standard. Paper cannot be hacked. Fireproof protects against physical disasters. A second copy in a different location protects against theft or destruction of the first. This is what serious holders do.

options.text: Saved in a password manager app on my phone
options.explanation: Password managers can be hacked, their servers breached, or your phone compromised. Digital storage of seed phrases is categorically riskier than physical. Even good security software has been breached before.

options.text: Memorized — I have a good memory and nothing physical can be stolen
options.explanation: Memory is not a reliable backup strategy. Illness, injury, stress, or simply time can cause anyone to forget. And if you forget, your crypto is gone forever. Physical backup is non-negotiable.

steps.microExplanation: Some people engrave their seed phrase on a steel plate to protect against fire and water damage. It sounds extreme — until you consider that the phrase may represent significant wealth with no recovery option.

steps.prompt: A website claims it can check if your wallet is safe by asking you to enter your seed phrase. It looks professional and even shows a green lock icon in the browser. What do you do?

options.text: Enter the phrase — it's a security check and the site looks legitimate
options.explanation: This is one of the oldest and most common crypto scams. No legitimate service, tool, or person ever needs your seed phrase. The green lock only means the connection is encrypted — it says nothing about whether the site is honest.

options.text: Close the tab immediately and never enter your seed phrase on any website, ever
options.explanation: Correct. This is a phishing site. Your seed phrase should only ever be entered into your wallet app itself — and only when restoring it on a device you control. No exceptions. No legitimate service needs it.

options.text: Enter just the first three words to see if it's trustworthy
options.explanation: Don't test it. Don't engage. The moment you enter any portion, you've signaled you might be vulnerable. And partial phrases can sometimes be used to narrow down the full one. Just close the tab.

steps.microExplanation: The rule is absolute: no website, no support agent, no tool, no friend ever needs your seed phrase. Anyone asking for it is trying to steal from you. There are no exceptions.

steps.prompt: Your friend sets up their first wallet. They're excited and message you a photo of their seed phrase to show you what it looks like. What do you tell them?

options.text: Cool! Keep it safe.
options.explanation: The phrase is now compromised — you've seen it, and if your message thread is ever breached, anyone could too. Your friend needs to create a new wallet immediately and transfer their funds. This is urgent.

options.text: Delete that message right now, generate a new wallet, and never photograph or share your seed phrase with anyone, ever
options.explanation: This is the right response. Photographs of seed phrases are a massive security risk. The phrase is now potentially stored on both phones, in both message apps, and possibly in a cloud backup. New wallet, now.

options.text: It's okay since you're a trusted friend — just don't share it with anyone else
options.explanation: Trust is irrelevant — security is about systems, not intentions. Even if you're completely trustworthy, your phone could be hacked, your messages could be leaked, or a cloud backup could be breached. The phrase needs to be retired.

steps.microExplanation: Seed phrases must never be photographed, screenshotted, emailed, messaged, or said aloud. The only safe mediums are physical: pen, paper, steel. Anything digital carries risk.

3.4.2.title: Spot the Scam
3.4.2.description: Crypto scams are everywhere. Learning to recognize them is essential for staying safe.
3.4.2.learningObjective: Recognize common crypto scam patterns (phishing, fake airdrops, rug pulls)

exerciseContent.scenario: You've been in crypto for two months. One morning you receive a Discord message from someone claiming to be from the official MetaMask support team. They say your wallet has been flagged for suspicious activity and will be suspended in 24 hours unless you verify your identity by entering your seed phrase on their verification portal. The message has the MetaMask logo and looks very official.
exerciseContent.question: What is happening here, and what should you do?

options.text: Verify quickly — a 24-hour deadline means this is urgent and real
options.explanation: Artificial urgency is a core manipulation tactic in scams. Scammers create time pressure to stop you from thinking clearly or asking someone else. Real companies do not cold-message you with 24-hour deadlines to hand over your credentials.

options.text: This is a phishing scam. MetaMask has no support team that contacts users directly. No legitimate service ever asks for your seed phrase. Report and block.
options.explanation: Classic phishing: official-looking branding + urgency + request for sensitive info. MetaMask is non-custodial software — they have no servers with your wallet info and no ability or reason to 'suspend' your wallet. Anyone asking for a seed phrase is a thief.

options.text: Ask them to prove they're from MetaMask before responding
options.explanation: Scammers can fabricate 'proof' — fake employee IDs, official-looking email threads, even spoofed phone numbers. The safer approach is to simply never engage. There is no valid scenario where a seed phrase should be provided.

options.text: Report it to MetaMask so they can investigate the security breach
options.explanation: Reporting is good, but the framing is wrong — MetaMask wasn't breached, you were targeted. The right steps are: don't engage, don't click any links in the message, report to Discord for abuse, and warn your community.

3.4.3.title: Security Best Practices
3.4.3.description: Protecting your crypto requires good habits. Here are the rules that matter most.
3.4.3.learningObjective: Know the actionable rules — 2FA, hardware wallets, verify URLs, never share keys

exerciseContent.instruction: Match each security practice with the correct reason it matters — and why skipping it creates real risk.

pairs.term: Enable 2FA (two-factor authentication) on every exchange account
pairs.definition: Adds a second layer of protection — even if your password is stolen, attackers still need physical access to your phone or authenticator app.
pairs.analogy: Like having both a key and a fingerprint scanner on your front door. Getting one doesn't get you in.

pairs.term: Use a hardware wallet for significant crypto holdings
pairs.definition: Your private key never touches the internet — even if your computer is fully compromised, an attacker cannot sign transactions without the physical device.
pairs.analogy: Like keeping your valuables in a safe that can't be opened remotely, no matter how sophisticated the hacker.

pairs.term: Always verify the URL before connecting your wallet to any site
pairs.definition: Fake sites clone the look of real ones exactly — a single swapped character in the URL (like 'uniswap.org' vs 'uni5wap.org') can lead to a wallet-draining attack.
pairs.analogy: Like checking the address on a building before walking in with your house keys.

pairs.term: Never share your private key or seed phrase with anyone or any service
pairs.definition: Your private key is the only proof of ownership. Anyone who has it controls your wallet completely — and transactions cannot be reversed.
pairs.analogy: Like giving someone the master key to every lock you'll ever own, permanently. There's no getting it back.

3.4.4.title: The Security Checklist
3.4.4.description: Let's consolidate everything you've learned about security into a personal checklist.
3.4.4.learningObjective: Consolidate all safety knowledge into a personal checklist

exerciseContent.instruction: You've learned everything about crypto security. Now sort these practices into the right category: Essential (must do always), Recommended (good to do), or Never Do (actively harmful).

items.label: Write seed phrase on paper, store offline
Share seed phrase with customer support
Use a hardware wallet for large holdings
Verify wallet address before every send
Click links from crypto giveaway messages
Enable 2FA on exchange accounts
Use a separate browser profile for Web3 activity
Reuse the same password across crypto platforms

4.1.1.title: The Vending Machine Analogy
4.1.1.description: Smart contracts are simpler than they sound. Think of them as digital vending machines.
4.1.1.learningObjective: Understand smart contracts as automated if/then agreements

exerciseContent.scenario: You walk up to a vending machine. You put in €2, press B4, and a chocolate bar falls out. No cashier. No approval. No waiting. The machine just does what it was programmed to do: take money, deliver product. Now imagine this same logic applied to a contract between two people — a digital agreement that executes itself automatically when conditions are met.
exerciseContent.question: Which statement best describes what a smart contract does?

options.text: It is a legal document that lawyers store on a blockchain for safekeeping
options.explanation: Smart contracts are not traditional legal documents. They are code — instructions that run automatically. No lawyers required.

options.text: It is a self-executing agreement: when a condition is met, an action happens automatically — no middleman needed
options.explanation: Exactly like the vending machine: insert coin (condition met) → chocolate bar releases (action executes). No human decides whether to fulfill the deal. The code does.

options.text: It is a type of cryptocurrency that companies use to pay employees faster
options.explanation: Smart contracts are not a currency. They are programs. Think of them as the rules of a deal, written in code, that run themselves.

options.text: It is an AI that reads contracts and makes decisions on your behalf
options.explanation: Smart contracts are not AI — they do not think or adapt. They are more like a calculator: completely predictable, doing exactly and only what they were programmed to do.

4.1.2.title: Code That Keeps Promises
4.1.2.description: Unlike a human who might forget or change their mind, smart contracts always do what they're programmed to do.
4.1.2.learningObjective: Understand that smart contracts execute automatically, without human intervention

exerciseContent.instruction: Some agreements can be fully automated by a smart contract. Others still require human judgment, trust, or legal authority. Drag each promise into the correct category.

items.label: Release payment when a package is confirmed delivered
Decide whether a car accident claim is someone's fault
Automatically renew a subscription on the 1st of every month
Negotiate the terms of a business partnership
Send a percentage of ticket sales to the artist the moment a purchase is made

4.1.3.title: Smart Contracts on the Blockchain
4.1.3.description: Smart contracts don't run on a regular server. They live on the blockchain with unique properties.
4.1.3.learningObjective: Understand that smart contracts live on blockchains, are transparent, and can't be changed

exerciseContent.instruction: Smart contracts have unique properties that set them apart from traditional software. Match each property with what it means in practice.

pairs.term: The contract runs on thousands of computers at once
pairs.definition: It cannot be shut down by any single company, government, or person
pairs.analogy: Like a rule written in a book that everyone in a city owns a copy of — no single person can rewrite or destroy it

pairs.term: The code is public and readable by anyone
pairs.definition: You can verify exactly what the contract will do before you agree to it
pairs.analogy: Like being able to read every clause of a contract before signing — except the clauses are written in code, not legal language

pairs.term: Once deployed, the code cannot be changed
pairs.definition: The rules are permanently locked in — no one can alter them after the fact
pairs.analogy: Like carving your agreement into stone rather than writing it on paper — no one can quietly edit it later

pairs.term: Execution is automatic — no human triggers it
pairs.definition: When the conditions are met, it runs — no approval needed from any person or organization
pairs.analogy: Like a thermostat that turns on heating when the room hits 18°C — it doesn't wait for you to notice and decide

4.1.4.title: When Smart Contracts Go Wrong
4.1.4.description: Smart contracts are powerful, but bugs in the code can have permanent consequences.
4.1.4.learningObjective: Understand limitations — bugs are permanent, code is law, 'The DAO hack' simplified

exerciseContent.scenario: In 2016, a project called 'The DAO' raised $150 million worth of Ethereum through smart contracts. It was meant to be a decentralized investment fund run entirely by code. Then a developer noticed something: a bug in the smart contract's code allowed someone to repeatedly withdraw funds before the contract updated its balance. The attacker drained $60 million. The smart contract didn't malfunction. It did exactly what it was programmed to do — the programming just had a flaw.
exerciseContent.question: What does The DAO hack reveal about smart contracts?

options.text: Smart contracts are fundamentally insecure and should never be used for large amounts of money
options.explanation: The DAO hack revealed a specific coding flaw, not a fundamental flaw in the concept. Smart contracts have since been used safely to manage billions. The lesson is about rigor, not avoidance.

options.text: Because smart contracts execute exactly as written and cannot be changed after deployment, a bug becomes permanent and can be exploited — 'the code is law' cuts both ways
options.explanation: The attacker broke no rules — the code permitted what they did. The immutability that makes smart contracts trustworthy also means bugs are locked in. This is why smart contract auditing and testing matter enormously.

options.text: Ethereum's developers were negligent and should have prevented the hack from the outside
options.explanation: This is actually a fascinating detail: Ethereum's developers did eventually intervene by performing a 'hard fork' to reverse the hack — which itself sparked huge controversy about whether blockchains should ever be changed. The debate about that choice continues today.

options.text: Hackers can easily break into blockchains and steal funds at will
options.explanation: The blockchain itself was not broken. The attacker exploited logic within the smart contract code. The ledger remained intact. The vulnerability was in the application layer, not the chain.

4.2.1.title: Smart Contracts at Work
4.2.1.description: Smart contracts are already being used in insurance, real estate, and supply chains.
4.2.1.learningObjective: Understand real applications — insurance claims, supply chain, real estate escrow

exerciseContent.scenario: A flight is cancelled. Normally you'd spend 45 minutes on hold with an insurance company, submit a form, wait 3 weeks, and maybe get reimbursed. With a parametric smart contract, the insurance company has pre-programmed a rule: 'If flight FR102 is cancelled, automatically transfer €200 to the policyholder's wallet.' The airline's system confirms the cancellation. The contract reads it. Your €200 arrives in minutes. No claim form. No call center. No waiting.
exerciseContent.question: Which aspect of smart contracts makes this kind of automated insurance possible?

options.text: Smart contracts can predict which flights will be cancelled using AI
options.explanation: Smart contracts don't predict anything. They read verified data from external sources called 'oracles' — in this case, official flight status data — and respond to what already happened.

options.text: Insurance companies program smart contracts to handle their phone calls automatically
options.explanation: Smart contracts handle logic and value transfer, not phone calls. The key shift is removing the human decision-making step — the contract executes when a verifiable condition is confirmed.

options.text: The if/then logic of smart contracts lets verifiable real-world events automatically trigger pre-agreed financial outcomes
options.explanation: This is parametric insurance: define the trigger (flight cancelled), define the payout (€200), verify the data (flight status API), execute automatically. No adjuster needed. Speed and predictability replace bureaucracy.

options.text: Smart contracts make insurance free because they eliminate all company costs
options.explanation: Smart contracts reduce certain costs — administration, claims processing — but do not make insurance free. Actuarial risk, development costs, and infrastructure still exist. The efficiency gains can lower prices, but they don't eliminate them.

4.2.2.title: Running a Smart Contract
4.2.2.description: Let's simulate triggering and executing a real smart contract from start to finish.
4.2.2.learningObjective: Experience triggering a simulated smart contract end-to-end

exerciseContent.instruction: Simulate a real estate escrow smart contract. You are buying an apartment. Instead of a notary holding funds, a smart contract manages the transaction. Walk through each step and make the right decision.

steps.prompt: You have agreed to buy an apartment for 250,000 USDC (a stablecoin). The seller wants assurance you have the funds before they transfer the deed. What should happen first?

options.text: Transfer 250,000 USDC directly to the seller's wallet immediately
options.explanation: Sending funds directly before receiving the deed would leave you with no leverage. If the seller disappears, your money is gone with no recourse.

options.text: Send 250,000 USDC to the escrow smart contract, which holds it until both conditions are met
options.explanation: The smart contract acts as a neutral custodian. Neither you nor the seller controls the funds — the contract does. This protects both parties.

options.text: Wait for the seller to send the deed before you do anything
options.explanation: The seller has the same concern in reverse — they don't want to hand over a deed without confirmed payment. That's exactly why an escrow contract solves this: both sides commit simultaneously.

steps.microExplanation: Escrow smart contracts hold funds in limbo — accessible to neither party until the agreed conditions are verified.

steps.prompt: The funds are locked in the contract. The seller must now upload the signed property deed to a verified document registry. The contract is waiting for confirmation. What does the contract do while waiting?

options.text: It automatically returns the funds to you after 24 hours if nothing happens
options.explanation: Smart contracts do exactly what they are programmed to do — no more, no less. If the escrow terms say 'wait for deed confirmation,' it waits. Timeout logic must be explicitly written in.

options.text: It does nothing — it simply holds the funds and monitors for the deed confirmation signal
options.explanation: Smart contracts are passive until their conditions are triggered. The contract is live and watching, but it will not act until the deed confirmation arrives from the external registry.

options.text: It contacts the seller by email to remind them to upload the deed
options.explanation: Smart contracts have no ability to send emails, make calls, or communicate outside the blockchain. They only respond to data that arrives on-chain or from pre-approved data sources.

steps.microExplanation: A smart contract is like a locked box with instructions. It holds its contents patiently and only acts when its specific trigger conditions are met.

steps.prompt: The seller uploads the deed. The document registry confirms it is valid. The smart contract receives this confirmation signal. What happens next?

options.text: A bank reviews the confirmation and manually approves the fund release
options.explanation: There is no bank in this process. That is the point. The smart contract itself is the final arbiter — once the condition is verified, execution is automatic.

options.text: The smart contract automatically releases 250,000 USDC to the seller's wallet
options.explanation: Condition met → action executed. No delay, no approval, no intermediary. The entire logic was locked in when the contract was deployed. The seller gets paid. You get the deed. Simultaneously.

options.text: You are asked to manually approve the payment before it is released
options.explanation: Your approval was given when you deposited the funds into the contract. The contract's job is to remove the need for ongoing human decisions — that's what makes it trustworthy.

steps.microExplanation: This is the power of smart contracts in high-stakes transactions: trustless execution. Neither party has to trust the other. They only have to trust the code.

steps.prompt: The transaction is complete. No notary. No bank transfer. No 3-week wait. A friend asks you: 'But what if the seller uploaded a fake deed?' What is the honest answer?

options.text: Smart contracts automatically detect fraud — they can't be fooled
options.explanation: Smart contracts cannot judge reality. They only verify what data arrives on-chain. If a fraudulent deed somehow passed through the document registry's verification process, the contract would still execute. The security depends on the quality of the external data source.

options.text: The contract relies on the trustworthiness of the external registry. If the registry is compromised, the contract can be fooled — this is the 'oracle problem'
options.explanation: Smart contracts are only as reliable as the data they trust. The 'oracle problem' is a real challenge in blockchain: how do you get trustworthy real-world data onto a chain? Reputable oracles (like Chainlink) exist to solve this, but it remains an important limitation to understand.

options.text: This is impossible because all documents uploaded to blockchains are verified by the government
options.explanation: Governments are not involved in most blockchain transactions. The verification comes from the specific oracle or registry the contract uses. Trust in that source is critical.

steps.microExplanation: Smart contracts are powerful inside the blockchain. Their weakness is at the boundary with the real world — where they depend on external data sources called oracles.

4.2.3.title: Marc's Lunch Moment
4.2.3.description: Can you explain smart contracts to a friend over lunch? This is your capstone test.
4.2.3.learningObjective: Capstone: explain smart contracts simply and correctly

exerciseContent.scenario: You're at lunch with your friend Marc. He just read something about smart contracts and asks: 'Okay, but what actually is a smart contract? Is it like a PDF agreement? Is it an AI? I don't get it.' You want to give him an explanation that is accurate, simple, and actually helps him understand — not one that oversimplifies or confuses.
exerciseContent.question: Which explanation would you give Marc?

options.text: It's basically a robot lawyer — AI that reads legal contracts and enforces them automatically
options.explanation: This mixes up two unrelated technologies. Smart contracts are not AI — they don't read, interpret, or adapt. They execute fixed logic. And while they can have legal weight, they are code, not lawyers.

options.text: It's a self-executing agreement written in code and stored on a blockchain. When the conditions you programmed are met, it runs automatically — no person or company needed in the middle
options.explanation: This is the clearest accurate description. It captures the key elements: it's code (not a document), self-executing (no human trigger), blockchain-based (decentralized), and conditional (if/then). Well done.

options.text: It's a way for companies to store contracts securely on the internet so no one can hack them
options.explanation: Secure storage is a side effect, not the point. The defining feature of a smart contract is automatic execution — the 'if this, then that' logic. Calling it storage misses the essence entirely.

options.text: It's like a Bitcoin transaction, but for contracts instead of money
options.explanation: This is too vague and not quite accurate. Bitcoin transactions move value. Smart contracts execute logic. They can involve value, but they're fundamentally different things. Ethereum was built specifically to run smart contracts — Bitcoin was not.

5.1.1.title: Fungible vs. Non-Fungible
5.1.1.description: To understand NFTs, you first need to understand what 'non-fungible' means — and it's simpler than it sounds.
5.1.1.learningObjective: Understand the core concept — interchangeable (dollar bill) vs unique (painting)

exerciseContent.instruction: The word 'fungible' sounds technical, but the concept is simple. Match each item with whether it is fungible (interchangeable) or non-fungible (unique), and understand why the distinction matters for digital ownership.

pairs.term: A €10 bill
pairs.definition: Fungible — any €10 bill is worth exactly the same as any other €10 bill
pairs.analogy: If you lend someone €10 and they give you a different €10 bill back, you've lost nothing. The specific bill doesn't matter.

pairs.term: An original Picasso painting
pairs.definition: Non-fungible — this specific painting is unique; no other object is identical to it
pairs.analogy: If someone gave you a perfect photocopy of a Picasso, you'd have something that looks the same but is worth infinitely less. The original's identity matters.

pairs.term: One Bitcoin
pairs.definition: Fungible — one BTC is worth the same as any other BTC; they are interchangeable
pairs.analogy: Like dollars, but digital. You don't care which specific Bitcoin you receive — they all have the same value and function.

pairs.term: A ticket to a specific seat at a specific concert
pairs.definition: Non-fungible — row C, seat 14, 8pm on June 3rd is not the same as any other ticket
pairs.analogy: NFTs work like concert tickets: each one represents something unique and specific. Swapping your front-row ticket for a back-row one is not an equivalent trade.

5.1.2.title: Digital Ownership Before NFTs
5.1.2.description: You've 'bought' plenty of digital things. But do you really own them?
5.1.2.learningObjective: Understand the problem — you don't 'own' your Kindle books, Spotify playlists, or game skins

exerciseContent.scenario: In 2009, Amazon customers woke up to find that copies of George Orwell's novel '1984' had silently disappeared from their Kindle devices overnight. Amazon had remotely deleted them — even though customers had paid for them — due to a licensing dispute with the publisher. The customers received refunds, but the book they thought they owned simply vanished. The same dynamic applies today: your Spotify playlists, your Steam games, your iTunes purchases. You paid. But you don't own them the way you'd own a physical book.
exerciseContent.question: What does the Amazon '1984' deletion reveal about digital ownership before NFTs?

options.text: Digital purchases are always more risky than physical ones — you should never buy digital goods
options.explanation: Digital purchases have enormous convenience advantages. The issue isn't digital vs physical — it's whether ownership is enforced on a platform's terms or on provable, verifiable terms that no company can override.

options.text: Amazon made a mistake that violated consumer protection laws and should have been prosecuted
options.explanation: Legally, Amazon had the right to do this under its terms of service. That's the uncomfortable part. When you 'buy' a digital item on a platform, you're usually buying a license to access it — not ownership of the file itself.

options.text: When digital 'ownership' depends on a company's servers and policies, what you actually have is a revocable license — not true ownership
options.explanation: This is the core problem NFTs were designed to address. A platform can change its terms, go bankrupt, face licensing disputes, or simply decide to remove your content. True ownership means the asset is yours regardless of what any company decides.

options.text: This problem was solved when cloud storage became widespread — your files are safe in the cloud
options.explanation: Cloud storage makes files accessible, but doesn't change who controls them. Your Google Drive files are subject to Google's terms. If Google suspends your account, you lose access. Storage location and ownership are different things.

5.1.3.title: How NFTs Work
5.1.3.description: An NFT is a smart contract that proves ownership of a unique digital item on the blockchain.
5.1.3.learningObjective: Understand that an NFT is a smart contract proving you own a unique digital item

exerciseContent.instruction: An NFT proves ownership of a unique digital item on the blockchain. Drag these steps into the correct order to see how the ownership proof is established.

items.label: A creator makes a digital file (art, music, video)
A smart contract is deployed that links to the file and records who owns it
The NFT is 'minted' — the token is written permanently onto the blockchain
The NFT is transferred to a buyer — the blockchain records the new owner's address
Anyone can verify the complete ownership history, publicly and permanently

5.1.4.title: Creating Your First NFT
5.1.4.description: Experience the NFT minting process in a safe simulation.

exerciseContent.instruction: Experience the NFT minting process. You are a photographer who wants to sell a limited edition of your best photograph as an NFT. Walk through each decision.

steps.prompt: You have three photographs to choose from. Which approach makes the most sense for your first NFT?

options.text: Mint all three photos as a single NFT to save on fees
options.explanation: Combining assets into one NFT muddies the ownership. Each NFT represents a unique item. If you want to sell three photos, you'd mint three NFTs — or create a collection. Bundling them compromises their individual value and transferability.

options.text: Choose your single strongest photograph and mint it as one NFT
options.explanation: Each NFT should represent one unique, coherent item. Starting with your best work and understanding the process before scaling up is the right approach.

options.text: Mint 1,000 copies of the same photo — more copies means more potential buyers
options.explanation: You can create editions (multiple copies of one work), but calling 1,000 identical copies 'rare' undermines value. Scarcity is part of what NFTs are meant to verify. An informed buyer checks the total supply before purchasing.

steps.microExplanation: The asset you mint should have clear identity — what it is, how many exist, who made it. These details are permanently recorded on-chain.

steps.prompt: The minting platform asks you to set a royalty percentage. This means you receive a percentage every time your NFT is resold in the future. What is a reasonable approach?

options.text: Set 0% — buyers prefer no royalties and it makes your work more attractive
options.explanation: While low royalties can attract buyers, 0% means you receive nothing from future sales — even if your work becomes significantly more valuable. One of NFTs' most powerful creator features is ongoing royalties.

options.text: Set 100% — you made the work, you deserve all proceeds from every future sale
options.explanation: A 100% royalty means buyers receive nothing if they resell — so rational buyers simply won't purchase. The secondary market would be dead. Royalties work because they are a reasonable share, not the whole.

options.text: Set something between 5–15% — common for creative work, balancing creator earnings and buyer incentives
options.explanation: The NFT market has converged on roughly 5–10% as a standard range. It's enough to meaningfully benefit creators while leaving room for resale markets to function. Some platforms set 2.5% as default; 10% is common for established artists.

steps.microExplanation: Royalties are encoded in the smart contract — forever. If your work appreciates in value, you benefit from every future sale automatically.

steps.prompt: You are ready to mint. The platform informs you that minting costs a 'gas fee' of approximately €8. What is this fee?

options.text: A subscription fee charged by the NFT platform for using their service
options.explanation: Gas fees go to the blockchain validators who process and confirm transactions — not to the platform. The platform may charge its own fee separately, but gas is a network cost.

options.text: A fee paid to blockchain validators for processing the transaction that writes your NFT onto the chain
options.explanation: Every action on a blockchain requires computational work. Gas fees compensate the network participants who do that work. When the network is busy, gas fees rise. Some platforms offer 'lazy minting' to defer this cost until a sale occurs.

options.text: An insurance fee that protects your NFT if it gets lost or stolen
options.explanation: Gas fees provide no insurance. Your NFT's security comes from controlling your private key — not from paying fees. If someone steals your wallet's private key, gas fees don't protect you.

steps.microExplanation: Gas fees are the cost of writing anything permanently onto a blockchain. They fluctuate based on network demand.

steps.prompt: Your NFT is minted. The transaction is confirmed on the blockchain. What can you now verify publicly?

options.text: Nothing — blockchain records are private to protect creator identity
options.explanation: Most blockchain records are public by default. Anyone can look up a wallet address and see its transaction history. This transparency is a feature — it allows buyers to verify authenticity.

options.text: The complete history: when the NFT was minted, by which wallet, and every transfer of ownership since
options.explanation: This provenance is permanent and tamper-proof. A buyer can verify that your NFT was created by you, has never been duplicated, and has a clean ownership chain. This is fundamentally different from a JPEG anyone can copy.

options.text: Only your personal information — name, address, and bank account — to verify you are a real person
options.explanation: Blockchain addresses are pseudonymous — they don't inherently reveal your identity. What's public is the wallet address, not personal information. This is a deliberate design choice.

steps.microExplanation: The blockchain is an open ledger. Your NFT's entire history — mint, sales, transfers — is permanently readable by anyone.

5.2.1.title: NFTs in Gaming, Music & Identity
5.2.1.description: NFTs are more than just digital art. They are being used in gaming, music, and identity verification.
5.2.1.learningObjective: Understand NFT use cases beyond profile pictures

exerciseContent.instruction: NFTs extend far beyond digital art. Match each real-world use case with the specific problem that NFTs solve in that context.

pairs.term: In-game items (swords, skins, characters)
pairs.definition: NFTs give players true ownership — they can sell or transfer items outside the game, even if the game shuts down
pairs.analogy: Like owning a physical trading card rather than a card that only exists inside one game company's database and disappears if the company closes

pairs.term: Music releases
pairs.definition: Artists can sell music directly to fans as NFTs, earning royalties on every future resale without a label intermediary
pairs.analogy: Like selling a limited pressing of a vinyl record that automatically sends the artist a cut every time it changes hands at a record fair

pairs.term: Event tickets
pairs.definition: NFT tickets can prevent scalping, verify authenticity, and allow creators to capture a percentage of resale value
pairs.analogy: Like a concert ticket that knows who bought it, can't be counterfeited, and automatically sends 10% back to the venue if resold above face value

pairs.term: Digital identity and credentials
pairs.definition: Diplomas, certifications, and professional credentials can be issued as NFTs — verifiable by anyone, impossible to fake
pairs.analogy: Like a university diploma that anyone can verify is authentic in seconds, without calling the registrar's office

5.2.2.title: NFTs and Creators
5.2.2.description: NFTs change the economics of being a creator. Royalties on resale are a game-changer.
5.2.2.learningObjective: Understand how NFTs change creator economics — royalties on resale, direct sales

exerciseContent.scenario: A musician named Sofia releases her new album as NFTs. She sells 500 limited-edition copies at €50 each, earning €25,000 directly — no record label taking 80% of revenue. She programs a 10% royalty into each NFT. Six months later, her music goes viral. Fans start reselling their NFT copies for €400 each. Every resale automatically sends Sofia €40. Over the following year, the same 500 NFTs are resold hundreds of times in total. Sofia earns thousands more without releasing any new work — from the secondary market alone.
exerciseContent.question: What does Sofia's story reveal about how NFTs change the economics for creators?

options.text: NFTs guarantee that musicians will always make more money than with traditional record deals
options.explanation: There are no guarantees. Sofia's story depends on her work becoming valuable enough to have an active resale market. Most NFT projects don't achieve this. The model is powerful — but not automatic.

options.text: NFTs allow creators to earn directly at first sale and then continuously from every future resale — cutting out intermediaries and creating long-term revenue streams
options.explanation: Traditional models: artist gets paid once, intermediaries capture resale value. NFT model: artist earns at first sale, and every future sale pays a royalty automatically via smart contract. This is structurally new.

options.text: The only advantage is selling directly — the resale royalty idea is technically impossible to enforce
options.explanation: On-chain royalties are enforceable through smart contracts on compliant marketplaces. The limitation is that not all platforms honor them — some NFT markets allow royalty bypassing. This is an active debate in the space.

options.text: NFTs eliminate the need for distribution, marketing, and audience building for artists
options.explanation: NFTs change how value is captured, not how audiences are built. Sofia still needs fans to discover and care about her music. The technology is the infrastructure — the artist still does the creative and promotional work.

5.2.3.title: The Ownership Question
5.2.3.description: When you buy an NFT, what do you actually own? The answer might surprise you.
5.2.3.learningObjective: Understand what you actually own with an NFT — the token, not necessarily the file

exerciseContent.scenario: You buy an NFT of a digital artwork for €500. The image is stored on a server. You own the NFT — a token on the blockchain proving your ownership. Three months later, the company that hosted the image goes bankrupt. Their servers go offline. The image your NFT points to is now a broken link. The token still exists in your wallet. But the image it references is gone.
exerciseContent.question: What does this scenario reveal about what you actually own when you buy an NFT?

options.text: You own the image file itself — it is stored inside your NFT on the blockchain
options.explanation: Most NFTs do not store the actual image on-chain — the blockchain stores a token and a link (URL) to the image, which typically lives on a regular server or IPFS. This is a common misconception with real consequences.

options.text: You own the token — a verifiable record of ownership — but what that token points to depends on how the image is stored
options.explanation: This is the honest answer. The NFT is the certificate; the image is what the certificate points to. If the image is on a centralized server that goes down, the certificate points to nothing. Better NFT projects store images on decentralized storage (IPFS or Arweave) to mitigate this risk.

options.text: You own full copyright over the artwork, including the right to reproduce and sell it commercially
options.explanation: Buying an NFT does not automatically transfer copyright. Copyright belongs to the creator unless they explicitly transfer it. You may own the token — proof of a specific digital artifact — but not the right to print it on t-shirts and sell them. Read the terms.

options.text: You own nothing useful — NFTs are just receipt tokens with no real-world meaning
options.explanation: NFTs do provide real, verifiable provenance and ownership proof. The token itself has genuine value in contexts where provenance matters. The limitation is in how storage is handled — a solvable problem, not a fundamental flaw.

5.3.1.title: The NFT Boom and Bust
5.3.1.description: The NFT market exploded in 2021 and then crashed hard. What happened?
5.3.1.learningObjective: Understand the market cycle — 2021 hype, prices, crash, what survived

exerciseContent.scenario: In early 2021, a digital artwork by the artist Beeple sold at Christie's for $69 million. Overnight, everyone wanted an NFT. Profile picture projects ('PFPs') like Bored Ape Yacht Club sold for hundreds of thousands. People were flipping NFTs bought Monday for a profit by Wednesday. By late 2022, the market had collapsed. Many NFTs that sold for €10,000+ were worth less than €100. Trading volume fell over 95% from peak levels.
exerciseContent.question: What does the NFT boom and bust cycle teach us about emerging technology markets?

options.text: The crash proved that NFTs were always worthless and the entire concept has been permanently discredited
options.explanation: The internet also had a massive crash in 2000-2002. Amazon's stock fell 90%. That didn't mean the internet was worthless — it meant speculative excess had temporarily distorted prices. The NFT crash was similarly a correction, not an ending.

options.text: The boom-bust cycle reflects speculative excess layered over a genuine new technology — prices detached from utility, then corrected, but underlying use cases remained
options.explanation: This pattern repeats with most transformative technologies. Genuine innovation attracts speculation. Speculation inflates prices beyond fundamentals. Reality corrects. What survives the correction tends to be the actual utility. Some NFT use cases — gaming, ticketing, creator royalties — continue developing after the crash.

options.text: The crash was caused by governments banning NFTs, which destroyed the market
options.explanation: Governments explored regulation of NFTs in some jurisdictions, but the crash was primarily driven by speculative dynamics — rising interest rates, the collapse of the broader crypto market in 2022, and the simple exhaustion of speculative momentum.

options.text: Everyone who bought NFTs during the boom was deliberately defrauded
options.explanation: While some projects were deliberate scams, the majority of the boom was genuine speculative enthusiasm — people believed prices would keep rising. Speculation isn't fraud, even when it ends badly. Understanding the difference is important.

5.3.2.title: Spotting NFT Red Flags
5.3.2.description: Learn to recognize the warning signs of NFT scams and manipulated markets.
5.3.2.learningObjective: Recognize common NFT scam patterns — wash trading, rug pulls, fake collections

exerciseContent.scenario: You're browsing an NFT marketplace and you notice a collection with impressive stats: trading volume of €5M in the last 24 hours, floor price up 300% in a week, celebrity endorsements on social media, only 100 NFTs left available, and a roadmap promising exclusive real-world events and a token airdrop.
exerciseContent.question: Which of these signals should make you most cautious rather than excited?

options.text: The fact that only 100 NFTs are left — scarcity always increases legitimate value
options.explanation: Artificial scarcity is a manipulation tactic. Any creator can choose to mint 100 copies. The scarcity is only meaningful if there is genuine underlying demand — not manufactured urgency designed to pressure you into buying quickly.

options.text: The combination of sudden massive volume, celebrity endorsements, and urgency language — classic patterns of coordinated pump schemes and potential rug pulls
options.explanation: Wash trading (buying your own NFTs to inflate volume), paid celebrity promotions without disclosure, and artificial urgency are documented manipulation tactics. The combination of all these signals at once is a significant red flag. Projects with genuine long-term value don't need high-pressure tactics.

options.text: The roadmap — legitimate projects never make promises about future developments
options.explanation: Roadmaps themselves are not red flags. The questions are: Are the promises realistic? Are the team members identifiable and accountable? Is there evidence of progress? A vague roadmap with anonymous founders and no track record is concerning.

options.text: The floor price — NFTs that have risen in value are always a sign of manipulation
options.explanation: Price increases aren't inherently suspicious. Genuine demand can drive prices up. The issue is whether the volume and price movement is organic (real buyers for real reasons) or manufactured (sellers buying their own NFTs to create artificial activity).

5.3.3.title: What's Actually Valuable?
5.3.3.description: Not all NFT projects are equal. Let's build a framework for evaluating them.
5.3.3.learningObjective: Develop framework for evaluating NFT projects critically

exerciseContent.instruction: Not all NFT projects are equal — some are genuine innovations, others are speculative bubbles, and some are outright scams. Match each evaluation criterion with the right question to ask when analyzing an NFT project.

pairs.term: Team identity and track record
pairs.definition: Are the founders publicly known with verifiable credentials, or is the team anonymous?
pairs.analogy: You wouldn't give money to a business partner you've never been able to verify exists. Accountability matters — when things go wrong, anonymous founders simply disappear.

pairs.term: Utility beyond speculation
pairs.definition: Does owning this NFT give you something useful — access, rights, functionality — or is the only value that someone else might pay more later?
pairs.analogy: A train ticket has utility whether or not it appreciates in value. A collectible card may have value only if the market agrees. Know which one you are buying.

pairs.term: Smart contract audit
pairs.definition: Has the underlying code been reviewed by an independent security firm for bugs and malicious functions?
pairs.analogy: Like having a structural engineer inspect a building before you buy it. The contract may look fine from the outside but contain hidden problems that only an expert would catch.

pairs.term: Community quality
pairs.definition: Are the people interested in this project engaged with its genuine purpose, or primarily focused on price and flipping?
pairs.analogy: A neighborhood's character matters. A project whose community only talks about price tends to collapse when prices fall. One with engaged, purposeful members tends to be more resilient.

5.3.4.title: The Honest Take
5.3.4.description: Let's consolidate a balanced, honest perspective on NFTs — potential, limitations, and reality.
5.3.4.learningObjective: Consolidate a balanced view — potential + limitations + personal stance

exerciseContent.scenario: After completing this entire section on NFTs, you have a clearer picture. NFTs introduced genuine innovations — provable digital ownership, creator royalties on resale, new revenue models for artists. They also triggered one of the most speculative bubbles in recent financial history, attracted significant fraud, and left many buyers with near-worthless assets. Both things are true simultaneously.
exerciseContent.question: Which statement best captures a mature, honest perspective on NFTs?

options.text: NFTs were a temporary fad with no lasting significance — they will be forgotten within five years
options.explanation: The speculative bubble deflated, but the underlying technology — verifiable digital ownership and smart contract-enforced royalties — continues to develop. Gaming studios, musicians, and ticketing companies are building on these concepts with more pragmatic applications.

options.text: NFTs remain an exciting opportunity — anyone who buys now before the next wave stands to make significant returns
options.explanation: This is the kind of thinking that created the 2021 bubble. Past market cycles do not guarantee future returns. Any investment thesis based primarily on 'getting in before the next wave' is speculative, not analytical.

options.text: NFTs introduced genuinely useful concepts — verifiable ownership and creator royalties — while also enabling significant speculation and fraud. The technology is real; its best applications are still being discovered
options.explanation: This is the balanced take. New technologies rarely arrive fully formed. The internet's first decade was also messy, speculative, and full of fraud. The question is not whether NFTs were oversold — they were. The question is what legitimate utility persists. That's still being written.

options.text: The entire category should be avoided — the scam rate is too high for any legitimate use
options.explanation: Fraud exists in every financial market — stock markets, real estate, and venture capital included. High scam prevalence in early NFT markets reflects the lack of regulation and low barrier to entry, not an inherent flaw in the concept of digital ownership.

6.1.1.title: What is DeFi?
6.1.1.description: Imagine banking services — lending, borrowing, trading — without any bank. That's DeFi.
6.1.1.learningObjective: Understand decentralized finance — financial services without institutions

exerciseContent.instruction:
DISCLAIMER: This section covers decentralized finance (DeFi). This content is educational only and does not constitute financial advice. DeFi involves significant financial risk, including potential total loss of funds.

DeFi replaces traditional financial institutions with software. Match each traditional banking service with its DeFi equivalent to see what changes — and what stays similar.

pairs.term: A bank savings account paying interest
pairs.definition: A DeFi lending protocol where you deposit crypto and earn interest from borrowers automatically
pairs.analogy: Instead of a bank lending your deposits to others and keeping most of the interest, a smart contract does the matching and the interest goes directly to you — but there is no deposit insurance

pairs.term: A bank loan requiring a credit check and application
pairs.definition: A DeFi loan where you deposit collateral worth more than what you borrow — approved instantly by a smart contract
pairs.analogy: Instead of a bank deciding if you're creditworthy, you prove ability to repay by locking up assets worth 150% of what you borrow. No credit history needed. But if your collateral loses value, you can be liquidated automatically.

pairs.term: A currency exchange booth at an airport
pairs.definition: A decentralized exchange (DEX) where smart contracts swap tokens using liquidity pools
pairs.analogy: Instead of a currency desk with marked-up rates and a cashier, software maintains a pool of tokens and adjusts prices based on supply and demand. Open 24/7, no ID required.

pairs.term: A financial advisor managing your investment portfolio
pairs.definition: A yield aggregator protocol that automatically moves funds between DeFi strategies to maximize returns
pairs.analogy: Instead of paying someone 1–2% annually to manage your money, a smart contract continuously optimizes where your crypto is deployed — with no human judgment and no account minimum. And no fiduciary duty if it fails.

6.1.2.title: Lending & Borrowing Without a Bank
6.1.2.description: DeFi lets you lend your crypto to earn interest or borrow against your holdings — all through smart contracts.
6.1.2.learningObjective: Understand DeFi lending — liquidity pools, interest rates, collateral

exerciseContent.instruction:
DISCLAIMER: This simulation is educational only. Real DeFi lending involves significant financial risk including liquidation and smart contract vulnerabilities.

Simulate a DeFi lending experience. You hold Ethereum and want to access liquidity without selling your ETH. Walk through each decision carefully.

steps.prompt: You hold 2 ETH (worth €4,000) and want to borrow €1,500 in stablecoins without selling your ETH. A DeFi lending protocol requires 150% collateral ratio. What does this mean for your deposit?

options.text: You deposit €1,500 worth of ETH and receive €1,500 in stablecoins
options.explanation: A 150% collateral ratio means you must deposit 1.5x what you borrow. Depositing exactly what you borrow would be 100% ratio — most protocols would reject this or liquidate you immediately if ETH dropped even slightly.

options.text: You deposit €2,250 worth of ETH (150% of €1,500) to borrow €1,500 in stablecoins
options.explanation: Exactly right. The overcollateralization protects the lender — if you default, the protocol can sell your ETH to recover the loan. The buffer also absorbs price volatility before liquidation is triggered.

options.text: You deposit your full 2 ETH and can borrow as much as you want
options.explanation: There is always a borrowing limit tied to your collateral value. Depositing 2 ETH at €4,000 might allow you to borrow up to ~€2,666 at 150% ratio — but borrowing to the absolute maximum is very risky given price volatility.

steps.microExplanation: DeFi loans are overcollateralized because smart contracts cannot judge creditworthiness. The collateral is the only protection against default.

steps.prompt: Your loan is active. ETH's price starts falling. The protocol shows your 'health factor' dropping — a measure of how safe your position is. At what point should you be concerned?

options.text: Only when ETH drops more than 50% — that's a very unlikely scenario
options.explanation: ETH has historically dropped 50%+ multiple times. In DeFi, your liquidation threshold might be much closer — some protocols liquidate at 125% collateral ratio, meaning a relatively modest drop can trigger automatic liquidation.

options.text: As soon as your collateral value approaches the liquidation threshold — you should add collateral or repay part of the loan
options.explanation: DeFi liquidations happen automatically and often partially — the protocol sells enough of your collateral to restore the health factor, plus a liquidation penalty. Acting early avoids penalties and gives you control.

options.text: Never — DeFi protocols have insurance funds that protect borrowers from liquidation
options.explanation: Some protocols have insurance mechanisms, but they typically protect lenders, not borrowers. Liquidation is an automatic protection for the protocol, not for you. No DeFi loan guarantees protection from your collateral being seized.

steps.microExplanation: The 'health factor' is your margin of safety. When it approaches 1.0, liquidation is imminent. Monitoring your position is your responsibility — there is no customer service to call.

steps.prompt: While your loan is active, the protocol is earning interest paid by borrowers like you. Other users have deposited crypto into the 'liquidity pool' to enable your loan. Where does the interest you pay go?

options.text: To the protocol's founding team as revenue
options.explanation: Protocol fees vary — some go to governance token holders, some to a treasury, some to liquidity providers. But the majority of interest typically flows to the people who supplied liquidity. This is fundamentally different from a bank keeping depositor spreads.

options.text: To the liquidity providers — the people who deposited crypto into the pool enabling your loan
options.explanation: This is the key shift in DeFi lending. The 'bank' is replaced by a pool of depositors who collectively lend and collectively earn interest. The smart contract manages matching, rates, and enforcement. The intermediary margin is eliminated.

options.text: It is burned — destroyed to reduce the total supply of the token
options.explanation: Some protocols do burn a portion of fees to create deflationary pressure on their governance token, but the primary interest earned goes to liquidity providers. Burning fees is an additional mechanism some projects add on top.

steps.microExplanation: DeFi lending is peer-to-pool lending. You borrow from a collective pool; interest is distributed to everyone who contributed to that pool.

steps.prompt: You repay the loan and recover your ETH collateral. You're considering depositing your ETH as a liquidity provider to earn interest yourself. A friend warns you about 'smart contract risk.' What does this mean?

options.text: You might earn too much interest and owe taxes
options.explanation: Tax obligations on DeFi income are real and worth understanding, but this is not what 'smart contract risk' means in this context.

options.text: If the smart contract code has a bug or vulnerability, an attacker could drain the entire pool — including your deposit
options.explanation: This has happened repeatedly with real financial consequences. In 2021 alone, over $1 billion was stolen from DeFi protocols through smart contract exploits. Higher yields in DeFi often reflect higher risk, including this one. There is no FDIC equivalent.

options.text: Smart contracts automatically decrease your interest rate the more you deposit
options.explanation: Interest rates in DeFi are typically determined by supply and demand — more liquidity in the pool reduces rates, but this is a market mechanism, not a penalty. It's not what the term 'smart contract risk' refers to.

steps.microExplanation: Every DeFi protocol carries smart contract risk. Audits reduce risk but don't eliminate it. Diversifying across protocols and not depositing more than you can afford to lose are basic risk management principles.

6.1.3.title: Trading Without an Exchange
6.1.3.description: Decentralized exchanges let you trade crypto without creating an account or trusting a company.
6.1.3.learningObjective: Understand DEXs and how they differ from Coinbase/Binance

exerciseContent.scenario: You want to swap some Ethereum for a different token. On Coinbase (a centralized exchange), you create an account, verify your ID, deposit funds, place a trade, wait for matching, and the exchange holds your assets the entire time. On Uniswap (a decentralized exchange), you connect your crypto wallet, approve the transaction in your wallet, and the swap executes directly through a smart contract — in about 15 seconds. No account. No ID. No withdrawal wait.
exerciseContent.question:
DISCLAIMER: This is educational content only. Crypto trading involves significant risk of financial loss.

What is the key trade-off between centralized exchanges (CEX) and decentralized exchanges (DEX)?

options.text: DEXs are always better — faster, cheaper, and more private than any centralized exchange
options.explanation: DEXs have real advantages but also real disadvantages. Gas fees can make small DEX trades expensive. Speed depends on network congestion. Liquidity for less common tokens may be thin. Customer support doesn't exist. The right choice depends on your situation.

options.text: CEXs are always safer — they have regulatory oversight, insurance, and customer support that DEXs lack
options.explanation: CEXs have advantages, but 'always safer' is wrong. The collapse of FTX in 2022 — a regulated, well-regarded exchange — wiped out billions in customer funds. When you hold funds on a CEX, you're trusting the company. 'Not your keys, not your coins.'

options.text: CEXs offer familiarity and customer support but hold your assets on your behalf; DEXs give you direct control of your assets but require you to manage your own security with no safety net
options.explanation: This is the honest trade-off. CEX: convenience and support, but you trust a company with your funds. DEX: self-custody and permissionless access, but a mistake with your wallet is irreversible with no one to call. Neither is objectively superior.

options.text: DEXs are only useful for illegal transactions since they require no identity verification
options.explanation: This is a common misconception. All DEX transactions are permanently public on the blockchain — they are among the most transparent financial records that exist. Regulators can and do trace DEX activity. Anonymous doesn't mean untraceable.

6.1.4.title: The Risks of DeFi
6.1.4.description: DeFi is powerful, but it comes with significant risks that you need to understand.
6.1.4.learningObjective: Understand DeFi risks — smart contract bugs, impermanent loss, rug pulls

exerciseContent.scenario:
DISCLAIMER: This section covers DeFi risks. Educational content only — not financial advice.

A developer named Leo moved his life savings (€40,000) into a DeFi yield farming protocol promising 200% annual returns. Three months later, the protocol was exploited through a smart contract bug. €25 million was drained from the pool in a single transaction. Leo lost everything. He had no recourse. No insurance. No customer service. The smart contract did exactly what the exploit instructed it to do.
exerciseContent.question: Which statement most accurately captures the risk landscape of DeFi?

options.text: Leo should have diversified — spreading across 10 DeFi protocols would have eliminated the risk
options.explanation: Diversification reduces but doesn't eliminate risk. Smart contract bugs, systemic market crashes, and interdependencies between protocols mean diversification helps but doesn't create safety. And 200% APY on any single protocol is itself a major warning sign.

options.text: DeFi returns are fundamentally risk-free because smart contracts enforce the rules
options.explanation: Smart contracts enforce rules, but cannot guarantee those rules are correct. The risk isn't that the contract will cheat you — it's that the contract itself may have bugs that others can exploit. 'The code is law' means bugs are also law.

options.text: DeFi carries unique risks — smart contract bugs, extreme yield promises as warning signs, no consumer protections, and losses with no recourse — that require a level of technical due diligence most people don't apply
options.explanation: This captures the full picture. DeFi isn't fraudulent by design — it has genuine utility. But the absence of institutional guardrails, combined with complex code managing real money, creates a risk profile unlike anything in traditional finance. 200% APY should trigger immediate skepticism.

options.text: The problem was that Leo used an unregulated protocol — sticking to regulated DeFi platforms would have protected him
options.explanation: Most DeFi protocols are not regulated in the traditional sense — that's a defining feature of the category. 'Regulated DeFi' is largely an emerging concept. Regulation does not yet provide meaningful protection for most DeFi users.

6.2.1.title: DAOs: Organizations Without Bosses
6.2.1.description: What if an organization was run by its members through voting, with no CEO or board of directors?

exerciseContent.scenario: The Uniswap protocol — one of the largest decentralized exchanges — is governed by a DAO. Holding UNI tokens gives you voting rights. Anyone can submit a proposal: change trading fees, allocate treasury funds, add new features. A recent vote allocated $20 million from the treasury to fund developer grants. 30,000 wallets voted. No CEO made this decision. No board approved it. The protocol's direction is determined by its stakeholders through code-enforced voting.
exerciseContent.question: What is the most accurate description of how a DAO differs from a traditional organization?

options.text: DAOs are completely fair because every member has an equal vote regardless of how many tokens they hold
options.explanation: Most DAOs use token-weighted voting — more tokens means more voting power. This can replicate plutocracy (rule by the wealthy) rather than democracy. Some DAOs experiment with one-person-one-vote systems, but this is not the norm.

options.text: DAOs replace traditional hierarchy with token-based governance, where rules are encoded in smart contracts and decisions require stakeholder votes rather than executive authority
options.explanation: This is the essence of DAOs. The structure is enforced by code — not by trust in any individual. No CEO can unilaterally redirect the treasury. Changes require community votes. This is powerful but also slow and vulnerable to voter apathy and token concentration.

options.text: DAOs are just regular companies that happen to use blockchain for accounting
options.explanation: DAOs are structurally different — there is no legal entity, no management hierarchy, and decisions are made collectively by token holders. The governance mechanism itself is the organization, not just a tool used by one.

options.text: DAOs are illegal in most countries because they operate without regulatory oversight
options.explanation: The legal status of DAOs varies by jurisdiction — some US states (Wyoming, Tennessee) have passed DAO-specific laws. Most DAOs operate in regulatory grey areas. Illegal is too strong; the legal frameworks are still being developed.

6.2.2.title: Web3: The Next Internet?
6.2.2.description: Web3 promises an internet where users own their data and platforms are decentralized.
6.2.2.learningObjective: Understand Web3 vision — user-owned data, decentralized apps, token economies

exerciseContent.instruction: Web1 was read-only. Web2 is read-write but platform-owned. Web3 promises read-write-own. Match each concept with what it means for users and the internet's structure.

pairs.term: Web2: Your data is the product
pairs.definition: Platforms like Facebook and Google collect your activity data, build profiles, and sell advertising access — you use the service for free in exchange for your attention and data
pairs.analogy: Like shopping at a store where entry is free, but every item you look at is logged, analyzed, and sold to advertisers without a direct payment to you

pairs.term: Web3: User-owned data
pairs.definition: Decentralized identity and storage protocols allow you to control your own data — choosing what to share, with whom, and potentially getting compensated for it
pairs.analogy: Like owning the data about your shopping habits and deciding which stores can access them, rather than the mall collecting everything automatically

pairs.term: Web2: Platform lock-in
pairs.definition: Your followers, content, and history live on a platform's servers — if they ban you or shut down, you lose everything you built
pairs.analogy: Like building a restaurant inside someone else's property with no lease — they can evict you at any time and keep everything you built

pairs.term: Web3: Token economies
pairs.definition: Users earn tokens for contributing to a network — whether adding data, validating transactions, or providing liquidity — aligning user incentives with network health
pairs.analogy: Like getting equity in a restaurant every time you bring in a customer, rather than just paying for your meal

6.2.3.title: Blockchain in Your Daily Life
6.2.3.description: Blockchain is already quietly entering everyday life in ways you might not expect.
6.2.3.learningObjective: Understand near-term real-world integration — CBDCs, supply chain, digital identity

exerciseContent.scenario: Without knowing it, you are already interacting with blockchain-adjacent technology. Walmart uses blockchain to trace food from farm to shelf — a lettuce recall that once took days to trace now takes seconds. Estonia runs its national healthcare records on a blockchain-based system. Brazil, China, and the European Union are piloting Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) — digital versions of their national currencies issued directly by their central bank. Your next passport may be issued as a verifiable digital credential on a public ledger.
exerciseContent.question: What does this suggest about blockchain's relationship with everyday life over the next decade?

options.text: Blockchain will replace all existing systems — banks, governments, and companies will become obsolete
options.explanation: This is maximalist thinking. The more likely trajectory is selective integration — blockchain solving specific problems (provenance, identity, settlement) within existing institutions, not replacing them wholesale.

options.text: Blockchain is purely a financial technology — it will only matter to investors and cryptocurrency traders
options.explanation: Supply chain tracking, healthcare records, identity, and voting are all non-financial applications in active development. Blockchain's core property — tamper-resistant shared records — is useful wherever multiple parties need to agree on a truth without fully trusting each other.

options.text: Blockchain is already embedded in infrastructure you use, and its presence will likely grow — often invisibly — in supply chains, identity systems, and national currencies
options.explanation: This is the nuanced reality. Most people will interact with blockchain technology without realizing it — through CBDCs, digital identity, or supply chain verification. The 'blockchain is irrelevant' and 'blockchain changes everything' camps both miss this quieter, pragmatic adoption curve.

options.text: CBDCs and corporate blockchains are not 'real' blockchain because they are controlled by central authorities
options.explanation: This is a purist position that doesn't match practical reality. CBDCs and permissioned enterprise blockchains do make trade-offs — they are not fully decentralized. But they use blockchain technology to solve real problems. Understanding the spectrum between fully public and fully private blockchains is important.

6.2.4.title: The Skeptic's Questions
6.2.4.description: Every technology faces legitimate criticism. Engaging with skeptics makes you smarter, not weaker.
6.2.4.learningObjective: Engage with legitimate criticisms — energy use, scalability, regulation, adoption barriers

exerciseContent.scenario: A thoughtful colleague raises several objections to your enthusiasm about blockchain: 'Bitcoin uses more energy than Argentina. Ethereum had major scaling problems for years. Most crypto users are speculators, not people solving real problems. And regulators are going to crack down harder as this grows.' You want to engage with these concerns honestly rather than dismiss them.
exerciseContent.question: Which response best demonstrates genuine understanding of these criticisms?

options.text: All these criticisms are just from people who don't understand the technology — once they learn more, they'll see why blockchain is the future
options.explanation: This dismissal is intellectually lazy. The energy criticism has empirical basis — Bitcoin's proof-of-work is genuinely energy-intensive. Scalability was a real problem Ethereum spent years solving. Engaging seriously with criticisms is how you develop a credible view.

options.text: These are all legitimate concerns: energy use is real (though evolving with proof-of-stake), scaling was addressed but remains a challenge, speculation dominates current adoption, and regulatory clarity is still being established globally
options.explanation: This is the intellectually honest position. Energy: Bitcoin is energy-intensive, but Ethereum's move to proof-of-stake cut energy use ~99%. Scaling: Layer 2 solutions are helping but not perfect. Speculation: currently dominant in crypto markets. Regulation: genuinely uncertain and actively evolving. All true.

options.text: These criticisms were valid before, but all of them have been fully solved — blockchain is now clean, fast, mainstream, and legally compliant everywhere
options.explanation: This overcorrects. Progress has been made — especially on energy (Ethereum's merge) and scaling (Layer 2 networks). But 'fully solved' is far too strong. Bitcoin still uses significant energy. Regulatory frameworks remain fragmented globally.

options.text: The solution is to ignore skeptics — early internet adopters faced the same doubts and they were proven right
options.explanation: The internet comparison is often used to dismiss criticism rather than engage with it. Importantly: many internet skeptics raised valid concerns — about the dot-com bubble, about privacy, about monopolization — that proved prescient. Being right about the technology doesn't make you right about the criticisms.

6.3.1.title: The Full Picture
6.3.1.description: Everything you've learned connects together. Let's see the big picture of how blockchain concepts relate.
6.3.1.learningObjective: Synthesize the entire curriculum — how all concepts connect

exerciseContent.instruction: Everything you have learned fits together into a layered ecosystem. Place each major blockchain concept into the layer it belongs to — from foundational infrastructure to end-user applications.

items.label: Bitcoin — the original decentralized value network
Ethereum — programmable blockchain enabling smart contracts
Smart Contracts — self-executing code that runs on blockchains
Crypto Wallets — your interface to interact with blockchains
DeFi — financial services built on smart contracts
NFTs — unique digital ownership tokens for specific assets

zones.label: Foundation Layer — the base blockchains and their native tokens
Application Layer — tools and protocols that run on top of blockchains
Use Case Layer — specific products and services built for end users

6.3.2.title: Explain It Like You Get It
6.3.2.description: The ultimate test of understanding is being able to explain things simply and correctly.
6.3.2.learningObjective: Prove understanding by selecting the best explanations for core concepts

exerciseContent.scenario: Your parent calls and says: 'I keep hearing about blockchain but I genuinely don't understand what it is. Can you explain it to me in plain language? Something I'll actually remember?' You want to give them the clearest, most accurate explanation you can — without jargon, without oversimplifying to the point of being wrong, and without needing to explain for 20 minutes.
exerciseContent.question: Which explanation would you give?

options.text: It's basically digital money that the government doesn't control — like secret cash for the internet
options.explanation: This describes cryptocurrency, not blockchain — and even then, incompletely. Blockchain is the underlying technology. Not all blockchains have a currency. And framing it as 'secret cash' misrepresents both the privacy (it's often public) and the legality.

options.text: It's a shared record book that thousands of computers maintain together. No one person controls it, and once something is written, it can't be erased. People use it to track money, ownership, and agreements without needing a bank or company in the middle
options.explanation: This captures the three essential ideas: shared (decentralized), permanent (immutable), and useful for tracking value and agreements without intermediaries. It's accurate without being reductive, and understandable without a technical background.

options.text: It's a complex cryptographic data structure that uses distributed consensus mechanisms and hash functions to achieve Byzantine fault tolerance
options.explanation: Technically accurate, but completely useless for a non-technical person. Understanding something deeply enough to explain it simply is different from reciting technical terms. Your parent would hang up.

options.text: It's a way to make money online without going through a bank — lots of people are making a lot of money with it
options.explanation: This reduces blockchain to a speculation vehicle — misleading and incomplete. It would likely lead your parent toward bad financial decisions rather than genuine understanding. The technology is far more than a money-making opportunity.

6.3.3.title: Your Blockchain Confidence Score
6.3.3.description: Time for a final self-assessment. How well do you really understand blockchain?

exerciseContent.instruction: This is your final self-assessment — a victory lap covering the full curriculum. These questions are meant to affirm what you know. There are no tricks. You've earned this.

steps.prompt: From the foundations: what makes blockchain records different from a regular database?

options.text: They are stored in a single, heavily protected government server
options.explanation: The opposite — centralization is what blockchain was designed to avoid. A single protected server is still a single point of failure.

options.text: They are distributed across thousands of computers, making them nearly impossible to alter retroactively
options.explanation: You've got it. The power of blockchain is in this distribution. No single actor controls the ledger. Changing one record would require changing thousands of copies simultaneously — practically impossible.

options.text: They are encrypted so that only authorized users can read them
